name: Cuenca del Cadagua Trail
name_original: Nacimiento del Cadagua y Siones de Mena — circular por el Valle de Mena
description: Jog the moderate 7.5-mile Cuenca del Cadagua Trail, gaining 634 feet of elevation through scenic river valleys and past historic landmarks.
region_intro: Jogging routes around Valle De Mena offer a diverse range of landscapes, situated between the Montes de Ordunte and the Montes de la Peña within the Cantabrian Mountains. The region features lush vegetation including beech and holm oak forests, interspersed with extensive green cattle pastures. Key natural features include the Cadagua River and its source, various waterfalls, and the Ordunte Reservoir, providing varied terrain for running. This geographical setting contributes to a rich environment suitable for different fitness levels. Best jogging routes around Valle De Mena The most popular jogging route is Cuenca del Cadagua Trail, a 7.5 miles (12.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 23 minutes to complete. This route leads through river valleys and past historic landmarks. Another top favourite among local runners is Villasana de Mena loop from Valle de Mena, a moderate 5.8 miles (9.3 km) path. This route passes through local villages and open countryside. Local runners also love the Old Bridge of Balmaseda loop from La Calzada, an 8.5 miles (13.7 km) trail leading along the Cadagua River, featuring the historic Old Bridge of Balmaseda, often completed in about 1 hour 25 minutes. Jogging around Valle De Mena is defined by river valleys, forested areas, and green cattle pastures. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy paths to more challenging trails with significant elevation gain. The routes in Valle De Mena are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 60 reviews. More than 700 runners have used komoot to explore Valle De Mena's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Valle De Mena?
A: Valle De Mena offers a wide selection of running routes, with over 70 options available. These routes cater to various fitness levels, from easy paths to more challenging trails.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on jogging routes in Valle De Mena?
A: The region is characterized by diverse terrain, including lush vegetation with beech and holm oak forests, extensive green cattle pastures, and river valleys. You'll find paths along the Cadagua River, around the Ordunte Reservoir, and through the foothills of the Montes de Ordunte and Montes de la Peña. Some routes offer significant elevation gain for those seeking a challenge.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners or families in Valle De Mena?
A: Yes, Valle De Mena has several easy running routes perfect for beginners or families. For instance, the trail around the Nacimiento del Cadagua (source of the Cadagua River) is an easy 3.3-mile path with gentle views. Overall, there are 4 easy routes available, ensuring options for a relaxed run.

Q: Can I find challenging trail running options in Valle De Mena?
A: Absolutely. For runners seeking a challenge, Valle De Mena offers 37 difficult routes with significant elevation gain. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24637147" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Kolitza Summit Shelter – Cima Gueci loop from La Calzada&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 1000 meters of elevation gain over 26 kilometers, providing a demanding trail running experience.

Q: Are there any scenic natural features or landmarks to see while running in Valle De Mena?
A: Many routes in Valle De Mena pass by beautiful natural features and historical landmarks. You can explore trails along the Cadagua River, discover various waterfalls like Peñaladros waterfall, or run around the Ordunte Reservoir. Historic sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/154436" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda&lt;/a&gt; are also accessible on some routes, offering picturesque views and points of interest.

Q: Are there any routes that feature waterfalls?
A: Yes, the region is known for its waterfalls. The Nacimiento del Cadagua (source of the Cadagua River) trail offers views of waterfalls and meadows. Other notable cascades include the Peñaladros waterfall near Cozuela and the San Miguel waterfall, providing beautiful natural sights along your run.

Q: Are there circular running routes in Valle De Mena?
A: Many of the running routes in Valle De Mena are circular, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. A popular example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10511209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villasana de Mena loop from Valle de Mena&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through local villages and open countryside. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40306059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda loop from La Calzada&lt;/a&gt;, following the Cadagua River.

Q: What do other runners say about their experience in Valle De Mena?
A: The running routes in Valle De Mena are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 60 reviews. Runners often praise the varied terrain, the tranquil atmosphere, and the beautiful natural environment, highlighting the region as an excellent destination for outdoor activities.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Valle De Mena?
A: Valle De Mena offers a pleasant environment for jogging throughout much of the year. The lush vegetation and diverse landscapes are enjoyable in spring and summer. The region's commitment to controlling light pollution also makes it a unique 'Star Park,' enhancing its natural appeal, especially during clear evenings.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural points of interest along the running trails?
A: Yes, several running trails incorporate historical and cultural points of interest. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1051328496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuenca del Cadagua Trail&lt;/a&gt; leads through river valleys and past historic landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/154436" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda&lt;/a&gt; is a notable historical feature accessible on some routes, and the small cascades formed by the Hijuela River are near an ancient Roman road.

Q: How can I access the running routes, and is parking available?
A: Many running routes in Valle De Mena are easily accessible, often starting from towns or villages within the valley. While specific parking information for each route varies, the presence of organized events like the San José Race, which traverses towns, suggests that parking is generally available in these areas. Public transport options might be limited, so planning your access by car is often recommended.

Q: Are there any specific routes that follow river valleys?
A: Yes, the Cadagua River is a significant feature of the region, and many running paths follow its course. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1051328496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuenca del Cadagua Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, leading through river valleys. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40306059" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bridge of Balmaseda loop from La Calzada&lt;/a&gt; also runs along the Cadagua River, offering scenic riverside jogs.

Q: What is the typical elevation gain on routes in Valle De Mena?
A: The elevation gain on routes in Valle De Mena varies significantly depending on the difficulty. Easy routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10511209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villasana de Mena loop from Valle de Mena&lt;/a&gt;, have minimal elevation gain (around 50 meters). Moderate routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1051328496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuenca del Cadagua Trail&lt;/a&gt;, can have around 190 meters of ascent. For difficult trails, you can expect over 300 meters, with some, like the Kolitza Summit loop, exceeding 1000 meters of elevation gain.
tips: Beautiful long in the middle of nature and where the Río Cadagua is born in the middle of the Montes de la Peña. Depending on the time of year, this birth can be found more or less full of water and where sometimes, you have to go to look for the birth a few meters below.

In the extreme north of the province of Burgos, almost bordering on Biscay lands and forming part of the historic region of Las Merindades, the church of Santa María de Siones constitutes, together with its neighbors of San Lorenzo de Vallejo and San Pedro de El Vigo. , the most important trio of Romanesque monuments in the Mena Valley.
The Velasco castle is a fortification of the Spanish municipality of Valle de Mena that can be seen from the outside with almost all of its entirety.

faqs.question: Where does the Cuenca del Cadagua Trail start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The trail traverses the upper basin of the Cadagua River, from Villasana to its source in the village of Cadagua. While specific parking details aren't provided, Villasana de Mena is a good starting point, and as a charming medieval town, it likely offers parking options for visitors.

faqs.question: Is the Cuenca del Cadagua Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail is generally suitable for outdoor activities like jogging, and many natural trails in Spain are dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash to protect the local wildlife, especially given the diverse fluvial ecosystem of the Cadagua River, which includes species like the European mink and various birds.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers picturesque scenery year-round. However, for comfortable jogging, spring and autumn typically provide milder temperatures and pleasant conditions. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring colder weather, especially in the upper reaches of the river basin.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there is no mention of permits or entrance fees required to access the Cuenca del Cadagua Trail. It is a natural route accessible to the public.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is diverse, featuring well-defined 'caminitos' (little paths) parallel to the river and paths between meadows. In its upper reaches, the river runs over limestone, forming a bed of slabs or large blocks of rock. Some sections are 'sendas pisteras,' suggesting relatively well-marked and wider paths suitable for jogging. It's a mix of natural, mixed terrain.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner joggers?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally considered to have a moderate physical rating with good visibility, making it easy to follow. While it involves some undulations (approximately 193 meters of elevation gain), it's manageable for joggers with a reasonable fitness level, making it suitable for beginners looking for a moderate challenge.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you can experience the Source of the Cadagua, the vibrant fluvial ecosystem of the Cadagua River, and the unique geological formation of the Diapiro de Mena. You'll also pass through charming meadows and encounter the foothills of the Peña mountains. Historical points of interest include Siones de Mena and Castillo de Los Velasco, as well as remnants of old mills and smithies.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: The fluvial ecosystem of the Cadagua River is rich in biodiversity. You might spot diverse plant and animal species, including endangered European mink, native trout, and various bird species such as the dipper and kingfisher.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route coincide with the GR-85, a long-distance path. It also covers parts of the Cuenca del Cadagua, Camino de Las Merindades, and Ruta de los Sentidos trails.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or accommodation near the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail passes through or near charming medieval towns like Villasana de Mena. These towns typically offer local amenities such as cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options for visitors.

faqs.question: What gear should I bring for jogging this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the diverse terrain, comfortable jogging shoes with good grip are recommended. Depending on the weather, layers of clothing, water, and perhaps a light snack would be beneficial. Sun protection is also advisable.

faqs.question: Is the Cuenca del Cadagua Trail a loop route?
faqs.answer: The provided tour data indicates this specific route is a circular trail, meaning it is a loop. This allows you to start and end at the same point without needing to arrange transportation back.
